Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd mostly wait until I felt like it or when something was due soon. Sometimes I'd listen to lecture recordings while doing other things, like walking to my part-time job or just lying in bed. I know I should have been more organized, but I just couldn't get myself to stick to any kind of schedule.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly try to catch up when a deadline was looming. It really depended on what else was going on in my life and whether I had the energy to deal with school stuff. Regular times weren't really my thing this semester.
Q3: Usually it was because an assignment was due or I got a notification about something. Sometimes my friends from the peer support group would mention something happening in class, and I'd log in to see what I missed. It wasn't like I woke up wanting to check the LMS—it was more reactive than anything.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. I tried at the beginning of the semester to make it a habit, but it never stuck. The only time it felt somewhat routine was when my peer support buddy would remind me about things, and we'd kind of check in together. But on my own, I just drifted away from it.
Q5: The audio recordings and video lectures were the most useful for me because I learn better by listening. I could play them while doing other stuff, which made it feel less overwhelming. The discussion boards were okay too because seeing what other students were confused about made me feel less alone in not understanding things.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and the textbook links. Reading on a screen is hard for me, and I don't have reliable internet at home to keep going back to them. The practice quizzes I also avoided because they stressed me out—I didn't want to see how much I didn't know.
Q7: If it felt like it would actually help me pass or if it wasn't too overwhelming, I'd do it. Activities that were short and audio-based felt worth my time. But if something looked like it would take hours and I wasn't sure it would even help my grade, I'd just skip it and hope for the best.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely skippable to me. Like, if it wasn't going to affect my grade directly, why bother? I already had trouble keeping up with the required work. Required activities at least had consequences, so I'd force myself to attempt them even if I did a bad job.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them offline since I don't have good internet access at home. I also borrowed a friend's notes sometimes and studied from those instead of going through the LMS materials. A lot of my studying happened away from the computer.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours listening to downloaded lectures on my phone while commuting or before bed. None of that would show up anywhere. I also talked through concepts with my peer support group in person, which helped me understand things better than just reading ever did.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd download the audio files or sometimes even screen-record videos when I had wifi somewhere, then study them later at home. So I might only show one login or one view, but I'd actually go over the same material multiple times on my own.
Q12: Around midterms, everything felt impossible. I was sad a lot and just couldn't bring myself to care about school. My attendance dropped because I couldn't even get out of bed some days. The peer support helped a little, but there were weeks where I just checked out completely.
Q13: Yeah, all of those things. I was tired a lot, confused by the material, and had my extracurricular activities and other life stuff competing for my attention. When I was overwhelmed, the LMS was the first thing I'd ignore because it felt like the easiest thing to put off.
Q14: So many times. I'd log in, click through some stuff, maybe start a video, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about personal problems or just feeling too sad to actually absorb anything. It was like going through the motions without really being there.
Q15: I avoided the bigger assignments a lot, even when I knew they were important. I think I was scared of failing at them, so not trying felt safer somehow. It's dumb, I know, but when you're already feeling low, facing something hard feels impossible.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only reason I did anything. I'd scramble at the last minute to submit something, even if it was incomplete or not my best work. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all. They were stressful but also the only thing that got me moving.
Q17: I didn't really seek out feedback much, and when I got it, it sometimes made me feel worse about myself. If the feedback was encouraging, it helped a little, but critical feedback just confirmed what I already felt—that I wasn't good enough. So I kind of avoided looking at it.
Q18: The course structure was pretty rigid, which didn't work well for someone like me who was struggling to keep up. Once I fell behind, it felt impossible to catch up. There wasn't much flexibility, so I just kept drifting further away from where I should have been.
Q19: Having audio and video options made things easier for me since that's how I learn best. But the lack of flexibility with deadlines and the heavy reading requirements made it harder. Also, not having reliable internet made everything more difficult than it probably was for other students.
Q20: I think sometimes you're trying to figure things out or looking for something specific but can't find it. Or maybe you're just anxious and clicking around without actually accomplishing anything. High activity doesn't mean you're being productive—sometimes it means you're lost or overwhelmed.
Q21: That makes sense to me because a lot of learning can happen outside the system. If someone has good notes, studies with friends, or uses other resources, they might not need to be on the LMS much. I wish that had been me, but I didn't perform well even with my offline studying.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or how you were feeling. They miss all the offline studying, the emotional struggles, and whether someone was genuinely engaged or just going through the motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort because you want to understand, not just because you have to. It's feeling connected to the material and the class. I wanted to feel that way, but most of the time I just felt disconnected and sad.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally this semester or how hard it was just to function some days. It wouldn't show the times my peer support friends helped me feel less alone, or the studying I did offline. It just shows clicks and submissions, not the whole picture of what I went through.
